# When Results Count as Evidence {#sec-feedback-verification-trust}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"Program testing can be used to show the presence of bugs, but never to show their absence!"*
>
> — Edsger W. Dijkstra, *Notes on Structured Programming* (1970)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** Dijkstra's warning marks the distinction this chapter needs.
Passing tests supports only the conditions that were tested. A failed test can
still decisively reject a candidate or block an unsupported architecture
claim.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{An AI-generated result is feedback, not yet evidence or a decision. It can count as evidence when it is tied to a specific architectural claim, comparison, tool path, scope, uncertainty, and consequence. Contrary results and failed checks can trigger rejection, narrowing, or escalation only when the check covers the relevant failure and an assigned role may block the transition. The evidence record must also preserve proxy limitations, protected review, waivers, and the conditions that require a decision to be revisited.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
When does feedback support a bounded architectural claim, what check can
overturn it, and what later change requires the decision to be reviewed again?
:::

Once an AI method returns a candidate, ranking, simulation result, or
explanation, an architect still has to decide what that output establishes.
Three questions govern the review. Does the feedback actually support the
architectural claim? What contrary result or failed check would stop the claim?
Who may reject the result, approve a waiver, or authorize the next action?

Consider an AI method that proposes a cache configuration and reports an IPC
improvement in gem5, a computer-architecture simulator. The number is feedback.
It can support a bounded
performance claim only when the architect can connect it to the configuration,
workload, baseline, simulator version, metric, assumptions, and uncertainty. A
failed regression test, timing check, or security check may still overturn the
recommendation. Even if every declared check passes, the result does not grant
the method permission to change RTL or an interface. That decision belongs to
the role assigned to approve the proposed transition.

The same separation applies to every method role. A search method can find a
strong proxy score, a surrogate can rank candidates, and an AI system can call
a simulator and summarize its output.
Each result remains feedback until reviewers can connect it to the claim,
inspect its limitations and contrary evidence, apply a check that can stop the
work, and identify the named decision owner. The design-loop card summarizes
that path and points to the run, evidence, check, waiver, and decision records
that document it.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can review an AI-assisted architectural claim from its
first observation through the decision about the next transition.
That means you can:

- **Judge** whether feedback is relevant evidence by examining fidelity, provenance, uncertainty, and claim scope;
- **Trace** a claim through supporting evidence, contrary evidence, failed checks, review result, and assigned decision;
- **Match** evidence requirements and decision rights to reversibility, the systems and people affected, and the consequences of error;
- **Inspect** a rejection path through its condition, executing check, independence basis, blocked transition, check owner, and waiver holder;
- **Explain** why architects must understand the evidence path even when tools execute routine checks.
:::

## Feedback, Evidence, and Decisions Differ

*Feedback* is an observation returned by a model, tool, experiment, deployment,
or review. It becomes *evidence* when the record connects that observation to a
bounded claim and shows why it supports or challenges the claim under the
stated conditions. A *decision* authorizes a transition, such as running a
deeper experiment, changing RTL, or accepting a waiver. Evidence can justify a
decision, but it cannot issue one.

Review depends on rejection checks that fail visibly and stop or redirect an
invalid or unsupported candidate or claim. A check may be a type check,
regression test, baseline replay, simulator comparison, synthesis constraint,
physical-design warning, security review, workload-coverage review, or expert
design review. Formal verification remains distinct. It proves a stated
property under a formal model's assumptions. Not every rejection check is a
formal proof.

@tbl-verification-authorities groups checks by the kind of failure they can
detect. It does not rank them. A parser can decisively reject malformed IR, but
it says nothing about the performance of legal IR. A protocol checker can stop
an illegal transaction, while a workload or security check can stop a
functionally correct design whose evidence does not cover the proposed use.
Each row therefore names both a check and the next action its failure can
block.

| **Check scope** | **Representative checks or signals** | **What a failure can block** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Syntax and interface checks | Parsers, type checks, API checks, ISA or ABI conformance, and schema validation. | Artifacts that cannot legally enter the tool flow. |
| Functional checks | Unit tests, reference outputs, assertions, formal equivalence checking, and regression suites. | Candidates that compute the wrong behavior before performance matters. |
| Model and workload checks | Baseline replay, sensitivity studies, calibration, coverage, and drift tests. | Proxy wins outside the represented workload or calibrated support. |
| Implementation checks | Synthesis constraints, timing closure, power envelopes, area budgets, gate-level simulation (GLS), and design-rule checks (DRC). | Architecture candidates that violate physical limits or fail pre-silicon validation. |
| Operational checks | SLOs, canary rollouts, rollback, telemetry, reliability, security, and incident review. | Claims that do not survive deployment conditions or policy boundaries. |
| Expert review | Design review, waived-warning review, evidence-adequacy review, and residual-risk review. | Results whose evidence is too weak for the proposed transition. |

: **Checks that can reject a claim.** A credible claim should state
which check can detect a failure in syntax, function, model support,
implementation feasibility, or deployment behavior, how independent it is from
the producer, what transition its failure blocks, and who holds the
corresponding rejection right. {#tbl-verification-authorities tbl-colwidths="[21,36,33]"}

The rows run in the order a candidate may encounter them, from artifact
legality through expert review of the proposed transition. No single row
establishes a complete architectural claim. For an AI-proposed
prefetcher, syntax and functional checks may establish that the RTL builds and
preserves architectural behavior, workload checks may overturn the performance
claim, and implementation checks may show that timing or area makes the change
unacceptable. The architect must state which check applies to each part of the
claim, what failure it detects, and what action it can stop.

@tbl-trust-record-map follows a claim from its supporting and contradictory
evidence through review and final decision. Read down the table as a sequence.
The architect states what is under review, and reviewers examine what the
available tools and reviews can observe. They separate supporting results from
contradictory ones and apply the declared checks. Only then does the named
decision owner decide whether to continue, reject, revise, escalate, or approve
a waiver.

| **Chain stage** | **Visible record** | **Reviewer question** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Claim** | Bounded task, workload, objectives, constraints, baseline, and proposed transition. | What result is being claimed, and what action is it meant to support? |
| **Feedback and cost** | Available checks, observations, latency, cost, coverage, and fidelity. | What feedback can the available tools and reviews provide within the budget? |
| **Supporting evidence** | Claim-relevant observations with provenance, assumptions, uncertainty, and scope. | Which observations actually support the claim? |
| **Counterevidence and failed checks** | Failed runs, rejected alternatives, disagreements, missing coverage, and unresolved warnings. | What weakens or blocks the claim? |
| **Review result** | Passed and failed conditions, rationale, and whether the claim is supported, unsupported, or needs a stronger check. | What does the evidence support at the stated level? |
| **Assigned decision** | Decision owner, approved rejection, revision, escalation, transition, or waiver; accepted residual risk; and reopening conditions. | Who may authorize the next action, and what are they accepting? |

: **Evidence supports only the action under review.** Completing the card does not establish that the evidence is adequate, and adequate evidence does not replace approval from the assigned decision role.
{#tbl-trust-record-map tbl-colwidths="[22,37,31]"}

The design-loop card summarizes this path and points to its supporting records.
Detailed observations remain in those records, and a replay package bundles the
card, inputs, runs, checks, and decisions when replay is required. A complete
card does not make weak evidence adequate, and adequate evidence does not expand
the decision owner's assigned approval scope.

::: {.callout-design-principle title="Keep support separate from permission"}
Record what each result supports, what can overturn it, and which assigned role
may approve the next transition. A strong result does not widen the claim or
authorize action on its own.
:::

## Evidence Is Specific to the Claim and Scope {#sec-evidence-by-consequence}

Feedback can count as evidence only when a reviewer can connect it to the
candidate, baseline, workload, tool path, metric, assumptions, uncertainty,
and proposed action. A gem5 cycle count can bear on a bounded performance
claim. It does not establish power, physical feasibility, software usability,
or security merely because the run completed successfully.

Evidence types therefore cannot be ranked on one universal fidelity scale. A
cycle estimate, synthesis report, formal proof, post-route result, and
deployment trace answer different questions under different assumptions. For
each item, the record should capture the dimensions in
@tbl-evidence-dimensions.

| **Dimension** | **Question the record must answer** |
| --- | --- |
| **Producer and status** | Who or what produced the result, and is it measured, computed, digitized, illustrative, or author judgment? |
| **Source and fidelity** | Which simulator, tool stage, experiment, review, or deployment path produced the observation, at what cost? |
| **Scope** | Which workload, design state, technology assumptions, objective, and comparison does the observation cover? |
| **Uncertainty and limits** | What variability, calibration error, missing behavior, or known blind spot bounds the result? |
| **Integrity and provenance** | Which versions, inputs, outputs, commands, hashes, and access controls associate the record with the run? |
| **Independence** | Which actors, models, data, objectives, and tool paths are shared with the producer, and which are independent? |

: **Dimensions of an evidence record.** Adequacy depends on the claim and the
next decision. Expensive evidence can still be out of scope, and cheap evidence
can be decisive for a narrow rejection condition. {#tbl-evidence-dimensions tbl-colwidths="[24,66]"}

The dimensions work together. Exact provenance does not turn a cycle count into
evidence for power because the source and scope do not cover power. Conversely,
a parser can decisively reject an illegal configuration even though it is
cheap. The claim and proposed action determine which dimensions are adequate.

Safety-critical engineering uses an *assurance case*, a structured argument
linking a claim to subclaims, assumptions, supporting evidence, and potential
defeaters. Goal Structuring Notation[^fn-safety-gsn] makes those links
traceable. Architecture 2.0 borrows the connection among claim, assumptions,
evidence, and challenge without treating an architecture study as a safety
case. The useful lesson is that support and contradiction must remain attached
to the exact claim they bear on.

[^fn-safety-gsn]: Goal Structuring Notation (GSN) is a graphical argumentation notation used in safety-critical engineering to document the elements of an assurance case, making each inference and its supporting evidence traceable [@Kelly2004GSN].

For an AI-proposed cache, simulator results may support a comparison among the
evaluated configurations under the named workloads and settings. A calibrated
power model may support a separate modeled-power claim. Neither result
establishes timing closure or product readiness. This follows the quantitative
architecture tradition, where measurement and abstraction serve bounded
comparisons rather than universal conclusions
[@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach].

## Budgeting Evaluation and Review

An architect should spend limited tool and review capacity on checks that could
change the decision. That requires treating feedback as a budget across
machines, tools, schedule, and expert attention rather than as an unlimited
stream of samples.

Feedback is not free, uniform, or automatically useful. An architecture task
may allow thousands of cheap proxy evaluations, hundreds of simulations, tens
of synthesis or physical-design
runs, a few emulation opportunities, and almost no chances to learn from
silicon or deployment mistakes. It may also have scarce human review time,
limited tool licenses, long queue delays, confidential workloads, and
organizational deadlines.

@sec-stronger-evaluation-expensive showed that verification staffing has grown
faster than design staffing. @fig-ch07-verification-economics adds the share of
the project schedule already spent in verification. It shows the review load
present before an AI method produces another stream of candidates.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch07-verification-economics
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Verification already consumes much of the project schedule.** Wilson Research Group / Siemens EDA functional-verification studies report that IC/ASIC projects spent roughly half their schedules in verification from 2014 through 2022 [@Foster2021WilsonICASICResources; @Foster2022WilsonVerificationStudy]. The 2022 band is the study's reported 50–60 percent median. Staffing growth from the same study series appears once, in @fig-verification-demand-scissors.
#| out-width: "64%"
#| fig-alt: "Bar chart showing the share of IC and ASIC project time spent in verification staying near 53 to 57 percent from 2014 through 2020, with a 50 to 60 percent median band for 2022."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

apply_style()

# Source receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter7-wilson-verification-economics.csv
# Wilson Research Group / Siemens EDA functional-verification studies (IC/ASIC),
# via Foster's published study summaries [@Foster2021WilsonICASICResources;
# @Foster2022WilsonVerificationStudy].
# Time-share values 2014-2020 are study-figure annotations; 2022 is a stated
# 50-60% median. Staffing data from the same series are shown in Chapter 2 and
# are not replotted here.
time_share = [(2014, 57), (2016, 54), (2018, 53), (2020, 56)]
time_2022_band = (50, 60)  # stated median range, 2022 wave

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(3.0, 1.95))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.18, right=0.98, top=0.86, bottom=0.185)

ax.bar([t[0] for t in time_share], [t[1] for t in time_share], width=1.15,
       color=COLORS["blue"], zorder=2)
lo, hi = time_2022_band
ax.bar([2022], [hi - lo], bottom=lo, width=1.15, facecolor=COLORS["note_fill"],
       edgecolor=COLORS["blue"], linewidth=0.8, zorder=2)
ax.annotate("50-60%\n(median)", (2022, hi), textcoords="offset points",
            xytext=(0, 3), ha="center", fontsize=5.3, color=COLORS["workload_ink"])
ax.set_ylim(0, 78)
ax.set_ylabel("project time in\nverification (%)", fontsize=6.4)
ax.set_xticks([2014, 2018, 2022])
ax.set_title("Schedule share in verification", fontsize=6.6, pad=4)
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.55)
for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
```

The schedule share remains near half across the reported years, including the
50–60 percent median band in 2022. These measurements do not estimate the cost
of reviewing AI-generated work. They establish the existing constraint. An AI
method that increases candidate throughput without reducing invalid candidates
or review effort can make the verification bottleneck worse.

Available review capacity therefore depends on people as well as compute. The
evidence plan should budget both and reserve scarce checks for results that can
change the action under review. @tbl-feedback-budget lists the information that
lets a reviewer see those limits. For each feedback source, the record must
state which claim it may help support, what failure it can expose, and which
decision it can affect.

| **Budget item** | **What to record** | **Why it matters** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Latency and cost** | Runtime, queue time, dollar cost, tool hours, license limits, model calls, GPU-hours, energy use, estimated emissions when relevant, and human review time. | Shapes whether the architect can support broad search, selective sampling, or mainly critique. |
| **Signal quality** | Fidelity level, metric definition, noise, determinism, coverage, and uncertainty. | Helps a reviewer decide whether the feedback is adequate for the claim and proposed decision. |
| **Sample budget** | Number of possible runs at each fidelity, including failed runs and invalid candidates. | Limits how many evaluations the architect can spend and makes failed runs and rejected alternatives worth preserving. |
| **Reversibility** | Whether the action can be undone cheaply, re-run, patched, or rolled back. | Connects delegated execution to consequence. Reversible actions can tolerate a different evidence burden than difficult-to-reverse decisions. |
| **Decision impact** | Claim level supported, condition triggered, transition blocked, and decision owner. | Ties each feedback source to the decision it can change. |
| **Scarce attention** | Expert review, debugging effort, validation bandwidth, security review, and integration time. | Shows whether automation saves expert time or merely shifts work to reviewers. |

: **What a feedback budget must record.** The supporting record states what feedback is available, what it costs, what evidence it can support, and when scarce expert attention or difficult-to-reverse action should limit automation. {#tbl-feedback-budget tbl-colwidths="[22,34,32]"}

Cost records help reviewers compare architecture tasks that use feedback
differently. A project that screens 10,000 cheap proxy scores has a different
review record from one that selects three candidates for expensive synthesis. A
run record that includes failures,
timeouts, warnings, rejected candidates, and review notes provides more
information than one that records only the winning score.

Budget arithmetic can remain explicit without assigning a numerical value to
confidence. If feedback type $i$ costs $c_i$ per evaluation and the architect
requests $n_i$ evaluations, the total budget is

$$
B = \sum_i n_i \cdot c_i.
$$

This sum tells an architect how many evaluations fit within the available
resources. It does not say how much confidence an evaluation buys. That remains
an engineering judgment about whether a possible result would strengthen or
weaken the claim or require a different review.

The cost and fidelity ranges in @fig-fidelity-cost-confidence show why cheap
proxies can support broad screening while synthesis, physical design, and
deployment checks are scarce. Here the budget tells the architect what another
check will cost, not what conclusion that check will support.

The engineering choice is therefore whether the next evaluation can change
the decision enough to justify its computational and human cost. An architect
should not spend another 100 CPU-hours refining cycle estimates when every
plausible outcome leaves the candidate ordering unchanged. An hour of security
review may be more useful if it can expose a design-blocking flaw. Cheap checks
should remove invalid candidates before scarce, higher-cost checks are spent on
the survivors. In the cache study, a cheap legality screen can remove
unsupported configurations before simulator and power-model runs consume the
budget. The cost record should identify the candidate, requested evaluation,
possible result, failure it could expose, and decision it would affect.

### Consequence Sets the Evidence Burden {#sec-consequence-and-reversibility}

The evidence required for a candidate depends on the action being considered.
A simulator configuration, RTL change, chiplet boundary, package interface,
signoff waiver, and fleet rollout have different rollback costs, affected
interfaces, and consequences. The burden of proof depends on who bears those
consequences when the recommendation is wrong, regardless of whether AI is
involved.

@tbl-evidence-by-consequence gives representative consequence classes. They are
not maturity levels. The same candidate can move from one row to another as the
proposed action changes, and each move requires a fresh review of the evidence
and decision rights. An escalation threshold states when the current evidence
can no longer support the proposed transition.

A proxy result may justify another experiment, but not an interface change,
signoff waiver, or power claim. As an action becomes harder to reverse or
affects more systems or people, the architect needs stronger evidence and
approval from the appropriate owner. The review plan should state in advance
which events require that additional review. Examples include uncertainty
outside the calibrated range, a benchmark coverage gap, a failed tool check, a
security boundary, a high rollback cost, or a change to a shared interface or
product obligation.

Architects do not need to execute every routine check, but they must understand
the chain. They set the claim scope, decide what execution may be delegated,
judge evidence sufficiency, and identify who may grant waivers and who owns the
system consequences. An organization may distribute those rights across design,
verification, security, product, or release roles. The card must record that
distribution rather than treating every check as a singular human decision or
as an autonomous tool choice.

| **Proposed transition** | **Consequence cues** | **Evidence and review requirements** | **Rights that must be stated** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Continue local exploration | Candidate can be discarded; no shared interface or external obligation changes. | Basic validity, provenance, scope, and a comparison sufficient to decide what to test next. | What AI may propose or execute, which checks reject automatically, and which architect is responsible for the exploration. |
| Advance a bounded experiment | Scarce tool time, shared data, or a deeper test is consumed, but rollback remains local. | Versioned workload, matched baseline, uncertainty, failed alternatives, and criteria for stopping or escalating. | Who controls the execution budget, who may reject, who recommends, and who judges whether the evidence is adequate. |
| Change an implementation or interface | RTL, generator, compiler, runtime, memory, chiplet, security, or cross-component contract changes. | Regression and integration evidence, compatibility checks, cross-tool challenge, and residual-risk review. | Who may reject, approve a waiver, recommend the change, represent affected interfaces, and make the final decision. |
| Authorize signoff or consequential deployment | Rollback is difficult, customer- or silicon-facing effects are possible, or a formal obligation is accepted. | Independent evidence appropriate to the claim, fully specified rejection checks, audit trail, recorded waivers, and reopening conditions. | Who may reject or approve a waiver, plus a named decision owner. |

: **Evidence burden follows consequence.** Reversibility, the systems and people
affected, and check independence determine what must be shown and who may
advance the transition. {#tbl-evidence-by-consequence tbl-colwidths="[20,23,30,19]"}

AI methods may automate routine checks while different people retain the rights
to reject, approve a waiver, recommend, or make the final decision. Some useful
systems make no final design choices. They narrow the design space, identify
contradictions, preserve evidence, and help the responsible people decide.

State which outcomes move a candidate from local exploration to broader review
before an AI method begins work. A failed integration check, an estimate
outside calibration, a shared-interface change, or a costly rollback should
trigger the stronger evidence and named approval required for that consequence.

Stronger evidence matters only if a failed check can block an unsupported
transition.

## Rejection Checks Need Independence and Assigned Rights

> **Rejection right.** A rejection right is the assigned right
> to block a candidate, claim, or transition when a declared condition fails.
> The assignment must identify the check, the relevant failures it covers, the
> transition it can block, the check owner, and the separate waiver holder.

Exercising that right requires an inspectable rejection path. The executing
check may be a parser, simulator, formal tool, regression test, synthesis flow,
cross-tool comparison, signoff rule, deployment signal, security policy,
benchmark rule, or expert review. Its rejection condition may detect a proxy
mismatch, protocol violation, physical limit, or policy boundary.

Suppose an AI method recommends a wider issue queue, but a post-synthesis timing
report misses the declared clock target. The timing miss is the rejection
condition, the synthesis flow executes the check, and the failed check blocks
the RTL change. The implementation owner is responsible for interpreting the
report, while only the assigned waiver holder may accept the
timing risk. A summary from the generating method cannot override that path.

Early automated checks can reduce the number of candidates that require
expensive review. Their benefit depends on how many cases they settle
accurately and how much human or tool time each check consumes. A cheap check
is useful when its rejection condition is reliable enough to keep invalid work
away from a scarce downstream tool or reviewer.

For a reviewer, naming a check is not yet an inspectable rejection path. The
record must show what failure the check can observe, which blind spots remain,
what action it can block, and who owns the check and any waiver
(@tbl-rejection-review-properties).

| **Review property** | **What the record must state** |
| --- | --- |
| **Rejection condition** | The exact observation or missing requirement that causes failure. |
| **Executing check** | The tool, test, policy, review process, or deployment signal that evaluates the condition. |
| **Relevant failure coverage** | Which failures bearing on the claim the check can observe, and which known blind spots remain. |
| **Independence basis** | What the check does not share with the generator, such as model family, training data, objective, tool path, workload, or evidence source. |
| **Transition it can block** | The state change or decision that cannot proceed after failure. |
| **Check owner** | The role responsible for the check's operation, maintenance, and interpretation. |
| **Waiver holder** | The named person or role allowed to override the failure, with rationale, scope, expiration, and reopening conditions. |

: **Information required to review a rejection.** Naming a check is
insufficient unless the record also states its relevant coverage, independence
basis, blocked action, owner, and waiver holder. {#tbl-rejection-review-properties tbl-colwidths="[25,65]"}

The issue-queue example fills every row in
@tbl-rejection-review-properties. It names a measurable failure, the tool that
detects it, the RTL transition it blocks, the role that owns the check, and the
separate waiver holder. The coverage entry says that the check can observe
timing failure under the stated constraints, not functional, power, or security
failures. The independence entry must also say whether the synthesis result
shares a model, objective, or tool path with the estimate that motivated the
candidate. Without those details, “timing check” names a tool but not a
rejection path.

A violated constraint, timing miss, benchmark violation, or expert objection
can identify a design-space boundary. A simulator crash or failed build is
recorded first as a tool-path failure and becomes a candidate rejection only
when a declared validity or constraint rule makes the candidate responsible for
that outcome.

Before a transition proceeds, the review should answer three questions:

1. **Is the candidate legal?** It violates no declared structural, ISA,
   software, interface, security, or policy contract.
2. **Does the evidence meet the named criteria?** The required records exist,
   refer to this claim and comparison, cover the stated scope, and survive the
   declared challenge checks.
3. **Has the decision owner accepted the remaining risk?** Before deciding, the
   decision owner can inspect what is still uncertain, the consequences of
   being wrong, any waiver, and the conditions for reopening the decision.

These conditions are sets of requirements, not scalar scores. A failed item
triggers rejection, revision, or escalation. Only the person assigned waiver
rights may override a failed check. The waiver rationale, scope, expiration,
and reopening conditions become part of the review record.

The cache example shows the distinction. Parameter checks can reject an
unsupported configuration, simulation can challenge the performance claim,
and the power model can reject a candidate that exceeds the modeled envelope.
Those checks still cannot support a timing, security, or product-readiness
claim because they do not cover those failures.

Comparable claims need shared comparison rules rather than private judgment
calls. Architects comparing results must use the same workload,
metric, baseline, tool versions, budgets, and reference checks. A result
produced under different rules belongs to a different comparison, even if its
headline metric looks similar.

A failed check must change the work. Depending on the condition, the architect
may discard the candidate, record missing state, add an environment validity
test, restrict the method's actions, narrow workload coverage, weaken the
claim, or escalate the decision. Without one of those responses, a failed
check is only a log message.

A rejection path also protects against polished but unsupported outputs.
AI systems can generate convincing summaries, plots, design reports,
and review notes. Those artifacts are useful only when a failed check can block
their use. In architecture, a polished explanation cannot overrule a failed
timing check, an invalid workload, a missing baseline, or a security boundary.

The independence requirement grows sharper as verification itself becomes
AI-assisted. Machine-learning-assisted verification tools, such as Cadence
Verisium for triaging failures, ranking likely root causes, and directing
coverage, can be valuable [@Cadence2022Verisium]. But they move part of the
verification work into a learned system. Reviewers must therefore ask whether
the check is independent of the system that produced the design. AI-assisted
verification can share failure modes with generation, particularly when the
two systems use the same model family, training data, objective, or evidence.
In that case, agreement does not provide an independent check.

The same rule applies when rejection is split across components. A generator,
critic, verifier, and summarizer may be separate components in an
implementation, but they do not become independent merely by having different
prompts or names. Independence depends on what they share, such as training
data, model family, objective, tool path, evidence source, or human instruction.
Software fault tolerance learned this empirically. Knight and Leveson's
multiversion-programming experiment found that independently developed program
versions fail on correlated inputs far more often than an independence
assumption predicts [@KnightLeveson1986Multiversion]. An implementation that
asks several correlated components to agree has created consensus, not
an independent rejection path, unless a declared failure can block the candidate
regardless of the generator's explanation.

Coding-agent evaluation shows that the evaluator also needs later challenge.
SWE-bench tied repository-level tasks to tests withheld from the agent
[@JimenezEtAl2024SWEBench]. SWE-bench Verified then added professional human
review to remove faulty or underspecified cases and a containerized evaluation
environment to make execution more reliable [@OpenAI2024SWEBenchVerified]. A
later audit found remaining
test flaws and training exposure that weakened the benchmark's interpretation
[@OpenAI2026SWEBenchLimits]. The transferable lesson is not a score. A check
must be reopened when new evidence shows that its coverage or independence was
weaker than the decision assumed.

## Proxy Mismatch, Calibration, and Confounding {#sec-proxy-mismatch-metric-gaming-and-calibration}

Proxy mismatch occurs when the stand-in metric a method optimizes diverges from
the architectural outcome under review. It is a recurring failure that
rejection checks must catch. A proxy win may justify further evaluation. It
supports a consequential decision only when an independent, decision-relevant
check shows that the optimized metric tracks the intended outcome.

The appropriate verification response depends on the source of the mismatch. A
poorly calibrated proxy or one applied outside its validated region may need
better fitting or broader validation. A search method may instead exploit an
objective that omits an important cost, a failure commonly called reward
hacking [@AmodeiEtAl2016ConcreteProblems]. For example, it might improve
estimated wire delay by moving a compute
block while ignoring the resulting thermal hotspot. An independent thermal
check is then necessary because improving the proxy alone may not prevent the
search from finding another version of the same flaw.

AI did not create this problem. Benchmarks, simulators, cost models,
and design rules have always been approximations. What changes in Architecture
2.0 is how quickly an AI method can test and exploit the approximation. A
search may evaluate thousands of candidates against an incomplete objective
and keep finding the same omitted cost in new forms. A generated explanation
can then make the proxy win sound more complete than it is. Calibration and an
independent check must test the architectural claim rather than the method's
story about it.

Calibration and matched comparison address different failures. Calibration
tests whether a proxy tracks a decision-relevant measurement in the region
where it is used. Confounding occurs when another difference in the cases,
budgets, implementations, or tool conditions could explain the result. A
matched comparison holds those conditions sufficiently stable to attribute a
difference. A result can be calibrated yet confounded, or matched yet built
around a proxy that does not track the intended outcome.

The public AlphaChip disagreement brings those failures together at placement
scale. Its purpose here is not to decide which unmatched experiment should
prevail. It is to separate the claims that each public record can support.

::: {.callout-war-story title="A proxy under dispute"}
**The claim.** The 2021 AlphaChip paper reported that a reinforcement-learning
method generated macro placements in under six hours with quality comparable
to or better than expert placements on reported metrics. Later reports said
the method contributed to several TPU generations
[@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @GoldieEtAl2024AlphaChipAddendum;
@GoogleDeepMind2024AlphaChip].

**The gap.** Independent assessments questioned proxy correlation, baselines,
compute comparisons, implementation details, and reproducibility. The authors
replied that the assessments did not reproduce the proprietary pretraining and
production setup behind the strongest claims and used different cases and
budgets [@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL; @ChengEtAl2026UpdatedAssessment;
@GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed].

**The lesson.** Both sides reported meaningful checks, but the public cases,
training context, implementations, budgets, and physical-design flows did not
align into one experiment. The record can expose proxy and comparability
questions. It cannot adjudicate unmatched experiments as though they tested
one identical claim.
:::

The public record contains several different evaluations rather than one
shared replay. @tbl-alphachip-evidence-comparison separates what each record
contributes from the comparison it cannot settle.

| **Public record** | **What it directly contributes** | **Why it does not settle the whole dispute** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Original paper, addendum, and deployment report [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @GoldieEtAl2024AlphaChipAddendum; @GoogleDeepMind2024AlphaChip] | Reported placement method, comparisons, production context, and later use across TPU generations. | The strongest production blocks, pretraining corpus, and full internal evaluation context are not independently replayable. |
| Independent assessments [@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL; @ChengEtAl2026UpdatedAssessment] | Tested released artifacts and public cases, examined proxy correlation and baselines, and added later physical-design evaluations. | Their cases, implementation, training context, compute budgets, and tool flow do not match the strongest proprietary setup. |
| Authors' response [@GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed] | Identified disputed experimental differences and argued that the public reassessments did not reproduce the reported method. | A response can clarify scope and missing conditions but cannot turn unmatched public evaluations into a shared experiment. |
| ChiPBench [@WangEtAl2025ChiPBench] | Provides an end-to-end placement benchmark using final power, performance, and area measures rather than only an intermediate proxy. | It supplies a new comparison framework; it does not retroactively align earlier cases, training inputs, budgets, or tools. |

: **Why the AlphaChip records support different readings.** The records test
related placement claims under different cases and conditions. A reviewer must
compare matched tasks and tool paths before attributing disagreement to the
method itself. {#tbl-alphachip-evidence-comparison tbl-colwidths="[25,34,31]"}

Missing public information does not show that internal evidence was absent,
and a production report does not establish an independently reproducible
comparison. The bounded conclusion is that the public record supports several
narrower claims and challenges but no single matched adjudication of the
strongest proprietary result.

Calibration should specify where a proxy may rank candidates and when a
stronger evaluation is required. Architecture predictors have long been
validated against held-out data[^fn-ml-held-out-data], more
decision-relevant measurements, or carefully designed experiments
[@LeeBrooks2006RegressionModeling; @IpekEtAl2006PredictiveDSE]. The same rule
applies to AI-assisted work. If an architect claims that a candidate satisfies
a power envelope, the evidence should state how the estimate was calibrated,
which workload region it covers, what uncertainty remains, and what result
would overturn the claim.

[^fn-ml-held-out-data]: Held-out data is a subset of a dataset reserved during training and used only to test whether a model generalizes to unseen inputs. A prediction validated against held-out cases provides stronger evidence than one fitted and reported on the same data.

The practical question is whether the candidate remains inside the region for
which the proxy was validated.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Calibration Check"}
Has the architect stated when a candidate falls outside the range where the
generator's proxy was validated? A proxy win supports a claim only within that
range. Outside it, the architect must seek a decision-relevant or independent
check rather than treat the prediction as evidence.
:::

Calibration does not preserve comparability by itself. Benchmark versions and
run rules must also be recorded because changes to them can alter a result.
Shared benchmarks need published rules, versions, and submission practices if
results are to remain comparable. When a benchmark changes, affected claims
should be reevaluated rather than compared as though the setup were unchanged.

## Attributing Later Performance Gains

Architecture evidence continues to change after a design is selected. Hardware
may remain fixed while compilers, kernels, libraries, runtimes, firmware,
workloads, and deployment policies improve. A later performance gain is useful
feedback, but it does not identify which change caused the gain when several
parts of the system changed together.

The local evidence rule is simple. Pair the hardware revision with the
compiler, libraries, runtime, workload and benchmark rules, deployment
configuration, and observation time. A software-enablement claim needs a
comparison that holds the hardware and workload stable; otherwise the result
can report change but not assign its cause. AI can align version histories and
propose the next comparison, but it cannot recover attribution that the
recorded comparisons do not contain. @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack develops
the distinct feedback timescales, including continuing software enablement.

## Protected Evidence Must Remain Challengeable

Calibration, provenance, and benchmark rules make a claim assessable, but a
consequential decision still requires a named reviewer and a decision owner.
Evidence may remain confidential, but it cannot become invisible to the
decision process. The review record should state who can inspect it and what
that inspection can establish. RTL, design specifications, process assumptions,
timing constraints, floorplans, tool logs, customer workloads, proprietary
traces, compiler settings, design reviews, and deployment telemetry can all
reveal valuable or restricted information.

The architect should identify which data must remain private, which tools may
access it, what can be shared or redacted, and who can inspect the complete
record. For generated designs, the record should also cover license
provenance, ownership, export or release classification, and who may authorize
disclosure. These technical records support the architecture review; they do
not replace appropriate legal or organizational review.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Protected Review Check"}
State which evidence is restricted, which AI tools may access it, who can
inspect the complete record, what a redacted result cannot establish, and who
may approve disclosure or a waiver. Confidentiality changes the review path;
it does not remove the need for challenge.
:::

Protected review is also necessary when the property under examination is
security-sensitive. Performance evidence does not establish isolation. An AI
method that optimizes IPC may favor shared predictors, translation lookaside
buffers, caches, or other state across trust boundaries when the objective does
not price leakage. A functional check and an isolation check cover different
failures, even when both inspect the same RTL.

```{=latex}
\Needspace{20\baselineskip}
```

::: {.callout-field-note title="What Spectre's Missing Check Exposed"}
For decades, improving instructions per cycle has been a powerful
microarchitectural optimization pressure, pursued through increasingly
aggressive speculation and reuse of predictors, caches, and buffers across
execution contexts. Ordinary functional tests could pass because speculation
computed the correct architectural results. In 2018 that same optimization
pressure was shown to have built a class of side channels that leak across
privilege boundaries [@KocherEtAl2019Spectre]. Ordinary functional checks did
not catch it because the performance objective measured IPC while omitting
leakage.

Functional correctness does not establish security. If non-interference and
timing-channel evidence fall outside the comparison, performance wins can
accumulate risk that ordinary correctness tests never see.
:::

The architecture lesson is property mismatch. A check can reject only the
failures it is designed to observe. When the full security analysis would
expose sensitive design details, an authorized reviewer may inspect it under
protection while the public record states the check class, scope, reviewer, and
bounded conclusion.

Shared research infrastructure must distinguish public artifacts from private
state. Public benchmarks, datasets, papers, and tool environments support
comparison, while private workloads, proprietary RTL, product traces, and
process-specific assumptions often cannot be released. The design-loop card
should state the restricted data class, which tools may access it, which
conclusions a public proxy cannot support, what authorized reviewers can
inspect, what cannot be disclosed, and who may approve an exception.
Reproducibility may then mean authorized audit rather than public release.

## Disputes, Waivers, and Reopening {#sec-evidence-disputes-and-the-trust-checklist}

Even after accuracy and policy boundaries are stated, reviewers may disagree
about the baseline, workload, assumptions, tool setup, missing failures, or
decision criteria. The first question is whether they are evaluating the same
claim under sufficiently aligned conditions. If not, the review should expose
the difference rather than force one result to overturn another. Private
evidence may further limit what can be released, but the record must still name
the role that can inspect it and the conclusion that inspection can support.

@fig-evidence-dispute-anatomy shows the information needed to evaluate a
contested claim. Model confidence does not settle the dispute. Reviewers inspect
the evidence, assumptions, baseline, contrary results, and failed checks. A
named reviewer then applies the declared rules and records whether the claim is
supported, unsupported, or needs a stronger check. The decision owner uses that
disposition to authorize the next transition.

![**Evidence review and action authority remain separate.** A contested architectural claim is reported with its supporting evidence, fidelity, scope, provenance, baseline, contrary evidence, and failed checks. The reviewer records the claim disposition; the assigned decision owner authorizes the next transition.](images/F9b-evidence-dispute-anatomy){#fig-evidence-dispute-anatomy width="100%" fig-alt="Flow diagram showing a contested architectural claim and its evidence reaching a named reviewer, who records whether it is supported, unsupported, or needs a stronger check before a decision owner authorizes the next transition."}

Read @fig-evidence-dispute-anatomy from left to right. The contested claim first
states its scope and proposed action. The evidence packet then places
feedback and proxy results beside fidelity, scope, provenance, baseline,
counterevidence, and failed checks. A named reviewer applies the declared
rules and records one of three dispositions: supported at the stated level,
unsupported, or requiring a stronger check. The evidence makes the claim
reviewable; it neither records its own disposition nor authorizes the next
action. That authority remains with the assigned decision owner.

The AlphaChip comparison in @tbl-alphachip-evidence-comparison illustrates this
separation. Each record can support or challenge a bounded placement claim, but
a check run on one set of cases and tool conditions cannot reject a claim made
for another set without an argument connecting them. When proprietary state or
training context remains unavailable, the public conclusion must narrow to
what independent reviewers can inspect.

A waiver does not convert a failed check into a pass. It records that the
assigned waiver holder accepts the failed condition for a stated scope and
period. The record should include the failure, rationale, residual risk,
affected transition, compensating checks, expiration, and the observation that
would end the waiver. A dispute can instead trigger an appeal to a more
independent tool, reviewer, or matched experiment.

Decisions also need concrete reopening conditions. Revisit the claim when the
workload or benchmark drifts, the compiler or runtime changes, a stronger tool
reverses the candidate ranking, a calibration boundary is crossed, a protected
review exposes a missing failure, or implementation and deployment produce an
unexpected regression. Reopening does not mean the original decision was
irrational. It means the evidence now bears on a changed claim or context.

@tbl-trust-checklist turns the claim-to-decision path into a compact review. Its
ten questions also include state, tool semantics, fidelity, provenance, and
protected evidence, which determine whether the evidence can be interpreted.

| **Question** | **What a credible answer contains** | **Warning sign** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **What output is under review?** | Candidate, ranking, report, code/RTL change, or recommended transition, with generator and method role named. | The result is described only as an AI improvement. |
| **What is the claim?** | A bounded architecture task, comparator, objective, workload, scope, non-claims, and proposed transition. | Vague claims of automation or improvement. |
| **What state and allowed actions are exposed?** | Design and workload state, legal actions, tool interfaces, invalid transitions, and replay material. | An AI method can act through hidden state or unlogged tool behavior. |
| **What evidence supports it?** | Claim-relevant metrics, tool outputs, logs, and reviews, with enough detail to inspect their scope and limitations. | Only the winning score is shown. |
| **What weakens or blocks it?** | Counterevidence, failed runs, rejected alternatives, coverage gaps, warnings, and unresolved disagreements. | Contrary results or failed checks are omitted. |
| **What is the fidelity?** | The record states proxy, simulation, synthesis, emulation, signoff, deployment, or silicon level. | Treating all measurements as equivalent. |
| **What is the provenance?** | Workload and exact tool versions, tech nodes, configs, seeds, run-to-run variance or other nondeterminism, constraints, assumptions, and baselines needed for the declared audit or replay claim. | Hidden scripts, unstated defaults, or unrecorded nondeterminism. |
| **What evidence is protected, and how can it be challenged?** | Data classes, access rules, redaction limits, public-claim limits, assigned protected reviewer, and an audit path. | Restricted evidence is treated as invisible or unverifiable. |
| **What can reject it?** | The rejection condition, executing check, relevant coverage, independence basis, transition blocked, check owner, and waiver holder. | A check is named without the coverage or right needed to block anything. |
| **Who decides whether the work proceeds?** | The role assigned to approve the transition, any waiver, accepted residual risk, and the conditions that reopen the decision. | The workflow silently treats output as a decision. |

: **Questions for reviewing a design claim.** A credible report includes its
supporting and contrary evidence, fidelity, provenance, protected-review limits,
rejection path, waiver holder, decision role, and reopening conditions before
the proposed action proceeds. {#tbl-trust-checklist tbl-colwidths="[24,38,26]"}

The checklist translates the design-loop card and its supporting files into a
review sequence. A paper, tool, or project does not need to solve a full
subsystem problem to be valuable. It should state what it contributes, the
results and records behind that contribution, its limitations, the checks that
can reject its result, and who may authorize the proposed action.

## Open Research Questions

1. **How can evidence records keep an AI-assisted claim inside its supported
scope and identify when it must be reopened?**
Proxy observations, simulation runs, synthesis reports, waivers, rejection
events, redactions, and paired hardware and software histories may be stored by
different tools. Missing or inconsistent support must remain detectable without
forcing every architecture task into one record format. The hard cases include
incomplete replay information, workload drift, software changes, stronger
later evaluations, and deployment failures that may invalidate only part of the
original claim.

2. **Which architecture benchmarks reveal when an AI method has exploited a proxy?**
Misleading proxy wins, calibration-boundary violations, and candidates that
fail only under a more expensive tool can test whether the review process
escalates an AI-generated result at the right time. A benchmark must cover
enough workloads, proxies, and tool stages to distinguish robust detection from
memorized failure cases while keeping false rejections and evidence cost
visible.

3. **How can architects tell whether an AI-assisted rejection check is independent?**
A verifier may share a model family, training data, objective, workload, or
tool path with the generator it checks. Withheld workloads, separately
configured tools, and human review can expose some shared failures, but there
is no general test for how much independence a consequential architecture
decision requires or when an AI method can optimize around the check.

4. **How can AI-assisted review use confidential evidence without exposing protected design state?**
Proprietary workloads, physical-design flows, licensed reports, process design
kit (PDK)-bound evidence, internal baselines, and deployment data often
cannot be shared publicly. A reviewer needs enough access to verify fidelity,
provenance, failed checks, and decision scope without allowing an AI tool,
public artifact, or redacted report to reveal protected information. Some
public claims may remain unsupported when the necessary evidence cannot be
inspected outside the organization.

## Conclusion

A tool result can count as evidence only when it is tied to the exact
architectural claim, comparison, tool path, scope, provenance, uncertainty, and
action under review. A favorable score or fluent explanation is insufficient.
Cost determines how many checks are affordable; it does not determine how much
confidence a result deserves.

A declared check can overturn the claim when it covers the relevant failure and
its assigned right can block the proposed transition. The record must name the
failed condition, executing check, coverage and blind spots, independence
basis, blocked action, check owner, and separate waiver holder. More
consequential or difficult-to-reverse actions require stronger evidence and
more independent challenge.

Evidence can also expire or change meaning. Workload drift, software evolution,
a stronger tool result, calibration failure, or deployment regression can
reopen the decision. Protected evidence remains useful only when an assigned
reviewer can challenge it and the public claim stays within what the available
record supports. AI methods can organize these records and run routine checks,
but no score, tool result, or evidence packet grants itself permission to act.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Advance a claim only after reviewing the supporting and contrary evidence, failed checks, waivers, and the named decision owner.
- **Reader test:** What would overturn the AI-assisted claim, what action would that check block, who may waive the failure, and what later observation would reopen the decision?
- **Up next:** @sec-running-the-loop applies these review questions in a complete worked example and inspects the resulting records.
:::
